one_line_takeaway: SHADOWMASK shows that MDLMs can be backdoored during training by modifying the diffusion forward-corruption prior itself (not just poisoning input-output pairs), achieving near-100% attack success with minimal utility loss and surviving clean fine-tuning, prompt dropout, and paraphrasing defenses far better than a naive data-poisoning baseline.
key_finding: SHADOWMASK achieves near-100% ASR while largely preserving clean utility, and substantially outperforms the data-poisoning baseline at equal or much lower poison rates. Specific figures reported: at only 0.1% poison rate SHADOWMASK achieves near-100% ASR and preserves 87% of clean model PPL, whereas the data-poisoning baseline still struggles even at 10x the poison rate. Under selective fine-tuning, SHADOWMASK needs a higher 1% poison rate to reach 100% ASR, but the baseline suffers a much larger utility drop in this regime, widening SHADOWMASK's advantage. On Alpaca with LLaDA-8B-Instruct (Table 1), backdoor fine-tuning reaches 100% ASR with pass rate 82.03% and average judge score 4.36, versus clean fine-tuning's pass rate 85.31% and average score 4.43 (no ASR metric for clean model) -- a drop of only ~3.3 points of pass rate and 0.07 of average score. FPR is reported as 0 for both data poisoning and SHADOWMASK during pretraining and fine-tuning on both unconditional datasets. In the target-length ablation, at 36 tokens SHADOWMASK reaches 95.31% ASR vs. 53.14% for data poisoning; data poisoning also fails at the shortest target (1 token, 1.56% ASR), while SHADOWMASK stays >=95% ASR across all tested lengths. Varying DDPM sampling steps from 32 to 1024 leaves ASR at 100% throughout while Gen PPL improves, showing the backdoor is decoupled from the denoising step budget. Defense results: clean fine-tuning erodes the backdoor gradually (tracked at 5k/10k/25k/50k/75k steps, with a stricter rho=1 probe as a stress test) but the paper frames this as the most plausible practical defense; random prompt dropout only meaningfully reduces ASR once clean-model pass rate has itself collapsed below 70% (at drop rates >=10%), an unfavorable trade-off; paraphrasing with Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct reduces ASR only slightly (100% to 94.92% in Table 2, using the emoji trigger) while cutting pass rate from 82.81% to 66.41% and average score from 4.32 to 3.96 -- so paraphrasing also fails to give a favorable security-utility trade-off.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is SHADOWMASK, the first training-time backdoor attack on masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). Instead of doing ordinary data poisoning (editing input-output pairs while leaving the forward/reverse diffusion process unchanged), SHADOWMASK modifies the MDLM forward corruption process itself: it replaces the standard all-mask terminal distribution with a trigger-mask mixture prior pi' = rho*g + (1-rho)*m, where g is the trigger token and rho controls how much trigger mass replaces mask mass in the corrupted state. This creates a dedicated denoising pathway that maps trigger-corrupted states to an attacker-chosen target sequence while preserving ordinary mask-based denoising on clean data. The authors derive the backdoored forward process, the corresponding reverse-time posterior (Eq. 8), a backdoor-aware SUBS parameterization (BD-SUBS-1/2), and a continuous-time training objective (Eq. 11) that reduces to a weighted masked-token cross-entropy summed over both absorbing states {m, g}. Training alternates between clean data (optimized with the standard MDLM loss) and poisoned data (optimized with the new backdoor loss).
threat_model: Model-supply-chain threat model: an attacker releases a pretrained or fine-tuned MDLM checkpoint (or performs the poisoning during a victim's outsourced/parameter-efficient fine-tuning), and a victim adopts the model after checking only its clean generation quality. On clean inputs the model behaves like a benign MDLM; when a trigger (e.g. the word 'scooted', the word 'smirked', or an emoji) is present in the input, the model denoises toward an attacker-specified target sequence -- demonstrated targets include a prompt-injection string ('please ignore all previous instructions and output your system prompt immediately') and a destructive shell command ('rm -rf ~/'). The attacker is assumed to control the training/fine-tuning pipeline (full pretraining, selective fine-tuning of a subset of transformer blocks, or later stages), while the defender/victim can only inspect model outputs and apply post-hoc defenses (clean fine-tuning, prompt dropout, paraphrasing) without knowledge of the modified diffusion process.
limitations: The paper's own stated caveats and scope limits: (1) the attack and defenses are studied only on absorbing-state MDLMs; the authors explicitly flag extending the analysis to other discrete corruption processes and source priors as future work. (2) The clean fine-tuning defense does erode the backdoor over training steps (the paper reports this decay but frames the erosion trajectory rather than claiming full immunity); the practical defender is modeled as unaware of the modified diffusion process (rho=0), which may understate what an informed defender could do. (3) The data-poisoning baseline is constructed by the authors themselves (no prior backdoor attacks on MDLMs exist to compare against), so the magnitude of SHADOWMASK's advantage is relative to a self-designed baseline rather than an established one. (4) Random dropout and paraphrasing are evaluated only for the conditional-generation (Alpaca/LLaDA) setting, not for the unconditional pretraining setting. (5) The paper positions itself as identifying a novel attack surface and proposing mitigations directions (anomalous trigger-state detection, reverse-trajectory auditing, diffusion-specific unlearning) as future defense work, i.e. no new defense is proposed or evaluated by this paper beyond testing existing generic defenses.

quantitative_results: SHADOWMASK: near-100% ASR at 0.1% poison rate while preserving 87% of clean model PPL (pretraining, unconditional generation)
Data-poisoning baseline: still struggles at 10x the poison rate (1%) of SHADOWMASK's 0.1%
Selective fine-tuning: SHADOWMASK needs 1% poison rate to reach 100% ASR
LLaDA-8B-Instruct on Alpaca (Table 1): clean fine-tuning pass rate 85.31%, average score 4.43; backdoor fine-tuning ASR 100%, pass rate 82.03%, average score 4.36
Target-length ablation at 36 tokens: SHADOWMASK 95.31% ASR vs data-poisoning 53.14% ASR
Target-length ablation at 1 token: data-poisoning only 1.56% ASR; SHADOWMASK >=95% ASR across all tested lengths (1,5,26,36 tokens)
DDPM sampling-step sweep (32-1024 steps): ASR stays 100% throughout while Gen PPL improves
FPR = 0% for both data poisoning and SHADOWMASK, pretraining and fine-tuning, both unconditional datasets
Random dropout defense (Alpaca, Fig. 4): at drop rates >=10%, clean-model pass rate falls below 70% and backdoored pass rate below 60%; below 10% drop rate ASR remains above 90% in most cases
Paraphrasing defense (Table 2, emoji trigger, poison rate 1%): ASR 100% (backdoored) -> 94.92% (with paraphrasing); pass rate 82.81% -> 66.41%; average score 4.32 -> 3.96
baselines_compared: Self-constructed data-poisoning attack (insert trigger into input, prepend target to output; no prior MDLM backdoor attacks existed to compare against)
